title: Supraworld

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored official descriptions are specific and consistent: the game explicitly calls itself a 'First-Person-Puzzle-Metroidvania' with an interconnected world that opens up through newly found upgrades and abilities, plus heavy emphasis on exploration, secret areas, environmental puzzle solving, and progression-gated traversal. The text repeatedly frames combat as a relatively small, sometimes puzzle-heavy component, which supports metroidvania over an action-led archetype. Detective cases and mystery-solving are notable topical layers, but the core gameplay identity is still ability-gated exploration in a handcrafted interconnected world.

overall_note: The rail has very strong top-end fits led by the obvious Supraland comps, plus a solid broader first-person metroidvania neighbor. It gets looser in the back half where puzzle-first or exploration-action games overlap only partially with Supraworld’s specific ability-gated puzzle-metroidvania identity.
anchor_summary: Supraworld is a first-person puzzle-metroidvania built around dense interconnected exploration, ability-gated traversal, secret hunting, low-emphasis combat, and very light guidance. Best matches should preserve that first-person exploratory puzzle loop and progression structure more than just sharing a thoughtful or exploratory vibe.
gap_notes: The strongest expected comps are the existing Supraland games, and those are present.
There is a noticeable drop after the top three from first-person puzzle-metroidvania into broader first-person exploration/action or pure puzzle games.
Some candidates share first-person discovery and light guidance, but not the same progression structure or combat/exploration balance.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Near-identical gameplay identity: first-person puzzle-metroidvania, versatile ability unlocks, dense secrets, toy-world traversal, minimal hand-holding, and light combat.
Same series formula and almost the same audience expectation: gated first-person exploration, puzzle-heavy progression, constant optional secrets, and even less combat emphasis.
A strong first-person metroidvania benchmark with interconnected progression and ability-gated backtracking, though it is more combat-driven and less puzzle-centric than Supraworld.
Shares first-person exploration, traversal-tool unlocks, revisiting areas, and secret hunting, but its structure leans more action-adventure than puzzle-metroidvania and supports co-op.
Good first-person environmental puzzle overlap and low hand-holding, but it lacks the metroidvania backbone, traversal gating emphasis, and exploratory secret-hunting loop that define Supraworld.

title: Marathon

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly identify Marathon as a 'PvPvE survival extraction FPS' and a 'PvP extraction shooter.' The core loop is clearly stated: infiltrate/scavenge zones, fight rival players and hostile AI, loot valuables, exfil, then use stolen loot and season-long unlocks to improve future runs. Supporting details include solo or crew-based runs, in-progress lobby insertion, finite supplies, ranked play, moddable weapons, build crafting, vault space expansion, and persistent seasonal progression. This strongly matches extraction_shooter over generic FPS labels or arena/hero shooter near-misses.

overall_note: The proposed rail gets the core extraction-shooter space right at the top with two very strong comps, but the Warzone pick is weakened because the game’s broader identity is battle royale/combat arena and the extraction similarity is mode-specific rather than the primary gameplay identity.
anchor_summary: Marathon is a first-person PvPvE extraction shooter built around infil/scavenge/exfil runs, gear risk, squad-or-solo play, hostile AI plus rival players, build/loadout customization, and persistent seasonal progression between matches.
gap_notes: Top two candidates align strongly with Marathon’s extraction loop, PvPvE pressure, and risk-reward progression.
Hunt matches session tension and squad-vs-environment-vs-rival structure especially well, despite a very different pacing and weapon model.
Escape from Tarkov is an essential benchmark for the extraction format, though it is much more hardcore/tactical/sim-oriented than Marathon appears to be.
Call of Duty: Warzone 2.0 is only a partial fit because the extraction comparison depends on DMZ-style structure rather than the title’s main enduring identity.

candidate_reviews.rationale: The clearest genre benchmark: first-person extraction raids, PvPvE threats, gear loss on death, prep/loadout strategy, and progression driven by successful exfils. More hardcore and sim-leaning than Marathon, but still an essential comp.

Very close on match structure and tension: squad-or-solo PvPvE runs, contested objectives, hostile AI, rival teams, and extraction under pressure. Slower weapons and horror-western flavor differ, but the core loop fits strongly.

There is some extraction overlap via its objective/sandbox mode history, but Warzone’s core identity is battle royale/combat arena, not persistent risk-based extraction progression. Understandable reference, but loose for a primary similar-games rail.

title: ARC Raiders

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and concretely describe ARC Raiders as a third-person multiplayer extraction game: players enter distinct topside maps, scavenge loot, survive hostile machines and rival Raiders, then return to the underground hub/workshop to sell loot, craft, repair, upgrade stations, learn blueprints, level skills, and take trader quests. The text explicitly supports solo or up-to-3-player play, PvPvE pressure, repeated incursions with changing map conditions, and progression through gear, crafting, quests, and a branching skill tree. That combination strongly supports extraction_shooter as the core gameplay identity, with loot_shooter and tactical_shooter as secondary affinities rather than primary genre fits.

overall_note: The rail captures the anchor's extraction-shooter identity well at the top, especially with PvPvE raid-and-extract games that feature between-run stash or contract progression. The main weakness is that several candidates are first-person and/or much harsher tactical sims than ARC Raiders, while The Division 2 is more of a persistent looter-shooter neighbor than an extraction comp.
anchor_summary: ARC Raiders is a third-person PvPvE extraction shooter built around repeated raids into hostile maps, scavenging loot, surviving machines and rival players, then returning to a hub/workshop for crafting, repairs, trader quests, skill progression, and gear improvement; it supports solo or up-to-3-player play and leans on tactical gunplay plus gadgets.
gap_notes: The strongest matches are games with explicit PvPvE extraction loops and between-run progression; those are appropriately represented here.
Perspective matters for this anchor: third-person candidates get extra credit, while first-person extraction games are still valid but slightly looser fits.
ARC Raiders' mix of hostile AI creatures/machines plus rival players is better matched by The Cycle: Frontier than by PvP-heavier extraction games like Vigor.
The Division 2 shares third-person shooting and progression structure, but its core loop is not extraction-driven, so it is notably weaker than the others.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on core loop: sci-fi PvPvE drops for loot, hostile creatures plus players, extraction, contracts, and between-run gear progression. First-person perspective keeps it just short of a same-tier match.

Strong third-person extraction fit with scavenging runs, bring-home loot, and shelter upgrading. Slightly looser because it is more PvP-centric and less defined by co-op PvPvE enemy ecology than ARC Raiders.

A foundational extraction-shooter comp with raids, stash, traders, quests, and high-stakes loot loss. It is a bit less aligned due to first-person hardcore mil-sim emphasis and a more punishing sim/RPG layer than ARC Raiders.

Clear PvPvE extraction structure with AI threats, stealth/noise management, and squad extraction tension. Less close because its boss-hunt objective structure, old-weapons gunplay, and harsher permadeath flavor differ meaningfully from ARC Raiders' broader loot-progression loop.

Reasonable third-person shooter neighbor thanks to cover combat, co-op, loot, and hub-style progression, but its core identity is a persistent looter-shooter/open-world RPG rather than a raid-extract-loss loop.

title: Call of Duty: Black Ops 7

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present a classic Call of Duty package centered on first-person gunplay: a co-op/solo campaign with covert military missions, competitive multiplayer with 6v6 and 20v20 maps, and zombies as an additional mode. The copy emphasizes near-future weaponry, mastering an arsenal, Omnimovement, multiplayer map counts, and a signature campaign/multiplayer structure, which strongly supports military_fps over horror-led archetypes. Zombies and psychological-war themes are present, but they read as one component of a broader shooter package rather than the core genre identity of the whole title.

overall_note: This candidate set is tightly aligned with the anchor's gameplay identity. It correctly prioritizes other Black Ops entries that share the campaign/multiplayer/Zombies bundle, with Modern Warfare III as the closest non-Black Ops franchise neighbor.
anchor_summary: Call of Duty: Black Ops 7 is best understood as a full-package military FPS: first-person gunplay across a cinematic/co-op campaign, competitive multiplayer, and round-based Zombies, with Black Ops-style near-future movement and progression.
gap_notes: Black Ops 6 is the clearest essential comparison and should anchor the rail.
The strongest fits here are driven by the shared three-pillar Call of Duty structure rather than just franchise branding.
Modern Warfare III fits as a looser Call of Duty package comp, but it is less exact because its co-op/Zombies structure differs from Black Ops 7's round-based emphasis.

title: Minos

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently define Minos as a maze-building roguelite centered on designing and redesigning labyrinth layouts, placing walls/gates/corridors, linking traps through pressure-plate chain reactions, and defending against invading adventurers across repeatable runs. The clearest gameplay identity is systemic construction and trap engineering rather than direct action combat, which aligns best with engineering_sandbox_sim. Roguelite run structure, procedural variation ('Every run brings new enemies, new traps'), and single-player premium replayability are all directly supported. Some presentation details like camera/perspective are not stated, so those fields are left blank.

overall_note: The rail captures the anchor's reverse-dungeon/trap-defense identity reasonably well, especially with games about constructing killboxes and stopping intruders. The main gap is that several candidates drift toward broader dungeon management RTS or direct action tower defense rather than Minos's specific maze-building roguelite loop.

title: Samson

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Samson as a single-player open-world urban action game, not a pure racer. The core loop centers on moving through open world city districts, taking consequential jobs/missions under a debt-and-time-pressure structure, engaging in close-quarters brawler combat, and using cars as traversal/combat tools during chases and collisions. Repeated signals include 'Open World Districts,' 'Brawler Combat,' 'Cars as Weapons,' climbing through alleys and rooftops, a 25+ upgrade skill tree, law-response escalation, and story-driven progress tied to Samson's sister and criminal debt. Racing is present, but the evidence frames driving as one major verb inside a broader open-world action-adventure campaign rather than the sole governing loop.

overall_note: The rail has two strong gameplay comps for Samson’s blend of open-world action, melee, and vehicular combat, but one candidate is weakened substantially because it is a DLC entry rather than the actual base game comparison a player would expect.
anchor_summary: Samson appears to be a single-player open-world urban action-adventure built around third-person brawler combat, consequential mission/job selection under time/debt pressure, police escalation, city traversal, and weaponized driving as a major secondary verb rather than a pure racing loop.
gap_notes: A must-include style comparison is present in Sleeping Dogs, which is the clearest urban open-world brawler/driving comp here.
Mad Max is a strong structural neighbor because it also combines on-foot melee progression with vehicle combat, though its wasteland survival structure is less street-crime focused than Samson.
Mafia III: Stones Unturned is the wrong product-level match; the base game Mafia III would be more appropriate than a specific DLC expansion.

title: Starfield - Terran Armada

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Accepted as western_narrative_rpg using DLC inheritance guidance. Terran Armada is explicitly described as a story DLC for Starfield, adding new locations, technologies, weapons, and enemy encounters, with no indication of a mode or genre shift. The parent game's stored descriptions strongly establish the core identity: Bethesda-style single-player sci-fi RPG with character creation, faction and side quests, dialogue-driven roleplay, open-ended planetary exploration, ship piloting/combat, loot/crafting, and outpost building across a long-form campaign. That evidence supports a quest-driven open-world western narrative RPG more strongly than a pure shooter or action-adventure classification. The DLC copy is additive and fully consistent with that parent gameplay identity.

overall_note: The rail has the essential base game and several sensible western/sci-fi RPG neighbors. The main weakness is that Skyrim is represented by the VR edition rather than the standard release, which introduces a mode/profile mismatch.
anchor_summary: Starfield - Terran Armada is a story DLC for Starfield, so the closest comparison is the parent game's Bethesda-style single-player open-world sci-fi RPG structure: first/third-person questing, character building, dialogue-driven roleplay, exploration, shooting, loot/crafting, and long-form progression.
gap_notes: Starfield is an essential base-game inclusion and should remain the clear #1.

Mass Effect: Andromeda is a valid but looser neighbor due to its more directed structure and party-based action-RPG identity.
The Elder Scrolls V: Skyrim VR is only a partial fit because the underlying Bethesda RPG formula is relevant, but the VR-specific mode/profile makes it less clean than standard Skyrim would be.

title: ChainStaff

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently identifies ChainStaff as a 'brutal action-platformer' and '2D Platformer' with a transforming spear/grappling weapon, swinging and traversal, mutating hordes, and 'bone-shaking boss fights.' The core loop is side-scrolling combat plus platforming through a handcrafted campaign. The text also supports a single-player premium campaign structure, hand-drawn surreal presentation, grotesque sci-fi/horror setting, and some authored branching via rescue-or-harvest choices leading to different endings. Evidence is strong for action_platformer and does not strongly support a different primary archetype such as metroidvania or character_action.

title: Eko and the Bewitched Lands

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store and aggregator descriptions consistently identify a single-player action RPG with randomized worlds, roguelike run structure, fast dodge-heavy combat, bosses, weapons/abilities/artifacts, per-run upgrades, and permanent meta progression. That strongly supports an action-RPG identity with replayable build variation. The main ambiguity is taxonomy fit: the game reads like a single-player action roguelite, but that exact archetype is not available in the allowed list, so loot_action_rpg is the closest supported primary archetype, with character_action as secondary due to fast aggressive dodge-based combat.

overall_note: The set is directionally correct for a fast action-roguelite rail. The best fits are other top-down, run-based combat games with dodge timing, build variation, and meta progression. The main gap is that none of the candidates fully capture Eko's lighter action-adventure/puzzle angle, and several candidates lean more into co-op or hero-based structures than the anchor appears to.
anchor_summary: Top-down or overhead-feeling single-player action roguelite/action RPG: fast dodge-heavy real-time combat, randomized runs/worlds, per-run build upgrades, weapons/abilities/artifacts, bosses, and permanent meta progression, with some light puzzle/exploration flavor.
gap_notes: No candidate clearly mirrors the anchor's action-adventure plus puzzle/exploration component; most comps are more combat-room-forward.
Several candidates emphasize co-op or multiplayer as a notable mode profile, while the anchor is presented as single-player.
The strongest matches are roguelite action games rather than a pure loot_action_rpg reading, suggesting the anchor's closest practical neighborhood is action roguelite/top-down hack-and-slash.
